# Installation and Setup

This chapter gets Git running on your machine and configured the way
a researcher actually wants it. There are four jobs: install Git,
tell it who you are, set a few sensible defaults, and create an SSH
key so that later, when you connect to GitHub (Chapter 9), you never
have to paste a password. Do these once per machine and forget them.

A note on the terminal output below. Git behaves slightly
differently across operating systems, and this book refuses to
fabricate output. So the Linux blocks are run live on a Linux machine
(Git 2.34.1) and shown verbatim; the macOS-specific and
GitHub-account steps are run on the author's Mac and pasted with
secrets redacted; and the Windows steps, which cannot be run on the
build machine, are described from the official Git and GitHub docs and
flagged as not-locally-run. Where a command's output is identical
across systems, one capture stands in for all.

## Step 1: install Git {.tier-beginner}

Pick your operating system. The goal of this step is simply that the
next command works.

**macOS.** The cleanest route is Homebrew: `brew install git`. If you
have ever installed Apple's Xcode Command Line Tools you already have
a Git, though often an older one, so installing through Homebrew and
letting it take precedence on your `PATH` is worth it. (The author's
Mac uses the Homebrew Git throughout this book.)

**Linux (Debian / Ubuntu).** Git is in the system package manager:

```bash
$ sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install git
```

On Fedora or RHEL the equivalent is `sudo dnf install git`. Many
scientific Linux machines, including the one used to build this book,
ship Git already.

**Windows.** Download "Git for Windows" from
[git-scm.com/download/win](https://git-scm.com/download/win) and run
the installer. It bundles **Git Bash**, a small Unix-style shell, and
this book assumes you run Git commands inside Git Bash so that the
commands match across all three operating systems. The installer also
offers Git Credential Manager (accept it) and a line-ending setting
(see the pitfall below). These Windows installer screens are
documented from the official Git for Windows project and are not run
on the build machine.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
Windows steps in this chapter are documented from official docs and
flagged, never captured live, because the build machine is
Linux/macOS only. Everything else is real captured output. The
underlying Git commands are identical on all three systems; only the
installer and a couple of defaults differ.
:::

Whatever the platform, verify the install with one command. This is
real output from the Linux build machine:

```bash
$ git --version
git version 2.34.1
```

Any reasonably recent version (2.28 or newer) has everything this
book uses. The same command on the author's Mac (Homebrew Git) shows
a newer release, which is exactly what you want:

```bash
$ git --version
git version 2.53.0
```

Run it on your own machine and confirm you get a version line rather
than "command not found." The version number will differ; anything
2.28 or newer is fine.

## Step 2: tell Git who you are {.tier-beginner}

Git stamps every commit with a name and email. Until you set them, it
refuses to commit at all. This is the first wall most beginners hit,
so here it is on purpose:

```bash
$ git commit -m "First commit"
Author identity unknown

*** Please tell me who you are.

Run

  git config --global user.email "you@example.com"
  git config --global user.name "Your Name"

to set your account's default identity.
Omit --global to set the identity only in this repository.

fatal: unable to auto-detect email address (got 'adoring-youthful-fermat@claude.(none)')
```

The fix is the two commands Git just told you to run. Set them once
with `--global` and every repository on the machine inherits them.
Use your real name and an email you are happy to have public in the
project history (the same one you will use for GitHub):

```bash
$ git config --global user.name "Pat Researcher"
$ git config --global user.email "pat@example.edu"
$ git config --global --get user.name
Pat Researcher
$ git config --global --get user.email
pat@example.edu
```

Setting a value prints nothing; reading it back with `--get`
confirms it stuck. Substitute your own name and email.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The name and email are baked into each commit and travel with the
history forever; they are not a login and changing them later does
not rewrite past commits. If you commit a few times with the wrong
email (a common one is a throwaway address that does not match your
GitHub account, so your commits do not link to your profile), fix the
config now so future commits are right. Rewriting the email on old
commits is a history-rewrite operation (Chapter 6) and is rarely
worth it for a private project.
:::

## Step 3: set sensible defaults {.tier-beginner}

A handful of global settings save real pain later. First set the
defaults that are the same on every platform:

```bash
$ git config --global init.defaultBranch main
$ git config --global pull.rebase false
$ git config --global push.autoSetupRemote true
```

Then set the line-ending rule for your operating system. On macOS or
Linux, use:

```bash
$ git config --global core.autocrlf input
```

On Windows, use:

```bash
$ git config --global core.autocrlf true
```

Each line is silent on success. Taken together these settings make Git
behave the way the rest of this book assumes:

`init.defaultBranch main` makes every new repository start on a
branch called `main`. Since Git 2.28 the initial branch name is
configurable; if you leave it unset, Git uses `master` and nags you
about it every time you create a repo. Watch the difference. Before
the setting, `git init` prints a hint and uses `master`:

```bash
$ git config --global --get init.defaultBranch

$ git init demo-before
hint: Using 'master' as the name for the initial branch. This default branch name
hint: is subject to change. To configure the initial branch name to use in all
hint: of your new repositories, which will suppress this warning, call:
hint:
hint: 	git config --global init.defaultBranch <name>
hint:
hint: Names commonly chosen instead of 'master' are 'main', 'trunk' and
hint: 'development'. The just-created branch can be renamed via this command:
hint:
hint: 	git branch -m <name>
Initialized empty Git repository in demo-before/.git/
```

After the setting, the hint is gone and the branch is `main`:

```bash
$ git config --global init.defaultBranch main
$ git init demo-after
Initialized empty Git repository in demo-after/.git/
$ git -C demo-after symbolic-ref --short HEAD
main
```

`pull.rebase false` makes `git pull` merge by default, which is the
safe, predictable choice while you are learning; Chapter 7 explains
when you might prefer rebasing. `push.autoSetupRemote true` lets the
first `git push` of a new branch create its remote counterpart
without extra flags, which removes a common beginner snag in
Chapter 9. `core.autocrlf` is the line-ending setting, covered next.

You can see the whole assembled configuration at any time:

```bash
$ git config --global --list
init.defaultbranch=main
user.name=Pat Researcher
user.email=pat@example.edu
pull.rebase=false
push.autosetupremote=true
core.autocrlf=input
```

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
**Line endings differ across operating systems** and can make Git
report that an entire file changed when nothing meaningful did.
Windows ends lines with carriage-return + line-feed (CRLF); macOS and
Linux use a bare line-feed (LF). The fix is `core.autocrlf`: set it to
`input` on macOS and Linux (commit LF, leave your files alone) and to
`true` on Windows (check out CRLF, commit LF). This is one of the few
settings that genuinely differs by platform.
:::

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
`core.autocrlf` is a per-person setting, so it cannot guarantee that
collaborators on different operating systems behave the same way. The
durable, project-level fix is a `.gitattributes` file committed to
the repo with a line like `* text=auto`. Because it lives in the
repository, it overrides everyone's personal `core.autocrlf` and
makes line-ending handling part of the project, not the machine. Put
it in once and cross-platform coauthors stop generating phantom
whole-file diffs.
:::

## Step 4: set up SSH for GitHub {.tier-beginner}

You will not touch GitHub until Chapter 9, but the key you create now
is what lets you connect then without typing a password on every
push. The setup is: make a key pair, give GitHub the public half, and
test it.

### SSH or HTTPS?

GitHub lets you connect over either SSH or HTTPS. They are equivalent
in capability; the difference is how you authenticate. With **SSH**
you generate a key pair once, register the public key with GitHub, and
never think about it again. With **HTTPS** you authenticate with a
credential helper that stores a token: Git Credential Manager on
Windows and macOS, the macOS keychain (`osxkeychain`) on a Mac, or a
helper you install on Linux. This book uses **SSH** as the primary
path because it is the same on every platform and avoids token
juggling. If you prefer HTTPS, install Git Credential Manager and skip
to Chapter 9; the rest works the same.

### Create a key pair

`ssh-keygen` makes the pair. Use the modern `ed25519` type and put
your email in the comment so you can recognise the key later. This is
real output (the private key is never displayed, and the public
fingerprint is redacted here):

```bash
$ ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "pat@example.edu"
Generating public/private ed25519 key pair.
Your identification has been saved in ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
Your public key has been saved in ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
The key fingerprint is:
SHA256:REDACTED-FINGERPRINT pat@example.edu
The key's randomart image is:
+--[ED25519 256]--+
|           o +B.*|
|          o +=o*o|
|        o  ++o.+o|
|       . o o. oo.|
|        S o   .=E|
|         . o  ..=|
|        . . o  o*|
|        .o o ...@|
|        .o.+=  ++|
+----[SHA256]-----+
```

The interactive prompts for the file location and a passphrase are
not shown in the capture above (the key was generated
non-interactively so nothing secret was typed). On your own machine
you will see them: press Enter to accept the default location, and at
the passphrase prompt set a passphrase, which encrypts the private
key on disk, so use one. This writes two files: `~/.ssh/id_ed25519`
(the **private** key, which never leaves your machine and must never
be shared or committed) and `~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub` (the **public**
key, which is safe to hand out).

`id_ed25519` is just the **default** filename, not a law. If you ever
expect more than one key on a machine (a common case: separate work
and personal GitHub accounts), name them when you create them with
`-f`, for example `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_work`.
Then the rest of this section uses your chosen name in place of
`id_ed25519`. The next subsection shows how to find whatever you
actually have, rather than assuming the default exists.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The two keys are not interchangeable. You give GitHub the `.pub`
(public) file only. If you ever paste the file *without* `.pub`, you
have leaked your private key and must delete the pair and start over.
A private key is a secret on the same level as a password: never email
it, never commit it, never paste it into a chat or an issue.
:::

### Find the key you actually have

Do not assume `~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub` exists. On the author's Mac it
does not, because that machine uses named work and personal keys.
Asking for the default name by reflex just errors (real macOS output,
username redacted):

```bash
$ ssh-keygen -lf ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
ssh-keygen: /Users/<REDACTED_USER>/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub: No such file or directory
```

So list what is really there first, then fingerprint each one. This
is the real layout on that Mac (two named keys, fingerprints and
comments redacted):

```bash
$ find ~/.ssh -maxdepth 1 -type f -name '*.pub' -print
/Users/<REDACTED_USER>/.ssh/id_ed25519_work.pub
/Users/<REDACTED_USER>/.ssh/id_ed25519_personal.pub

$ ssh-keygen -lf ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_work.pub
256 SHA256:REDACTED-WORK-FINGERPRINT <REDACTED_COMMENT> (ED25519)
```

The lesson: find your `.pub` files, identify the one for this
account, and use that exact filename in the commands below.

### Give GitHub the public key

Copy the contents of the **public** key you just identified and add
it on GitHub under Settings, "SSH and GPG keys", "New SSH key". Using
the named work key as the example: on macOS,
`pbcopy < ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_work.pub`; on Linux use `xclip`; on
Windows Git Bash, `clip < ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_work.pub`. Substitute
your own filename. The GitHub web steps are documented here rather
than captured, since they happen in the browser on your own account.

### Test the connection

Once the key is registered, test it. This is real output from the
author's Mac, with the GitHub username redacted:

```bash
$ ssh -T git@github.com
Hi <REDACTED_GITHUB_USERNAME>! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.
```

The redacted name will be your own GitHub login. The "does not
provide shell access" line is not an error: it is GitHub confirming
the key works
and that SSH is only for Git, not for logging into a server. On the
very first connection ssh also asks you to confirm GitHub's host
fingerprint; type `yes`. If instead you see "Permission denied
(publickey)", the key was not registered correctly; recheck that you
pasted the `.pub` file.

## What you can now do

Git is installed, it knows who you are, it starts repositories on
`main` without nagging, it handles line endings sanely, and you have
an SSH key ready for GitHub. You have done the one-time setup that the
rest of the book assumes on every machine you work on. Chapter 5 puts
this to work: the daily loop of staging and committing in the running
example.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

On the machine you actually work on:

1. Run `git --version` and confirm you get a version line of 2.28 or
   newer. If not, install or update Git for your platform.
2. Set your name and email with `git config --global`, then read them
   back with `git config --global --get user.name` and `--get
   user.email`. Confirm they match the email you will use on GitHub.
3. Run the three shared sensible-defaults commands plus the
   `core.autocrlf` command for your operating system. Then run
   `git config --global --list` and find each setting you just made
   in the output.
4. Create an `ed25519` SSH key with a passphrase (name it with `-f`
   if you may end up with more than one). Run `find ~/.ssh -name
   '*.pub'` to list your public keys, then `ssh-keygen -lf <that
   file>` to read its fingerprint without revealing the key. Add the
   public key to GitHub and run `ssh -T git@github.com`; confirm the
   "successfully authenticated" line names your username.
